citations.apa: National Wine Agency of Georgia. (2025, January 20). Record revenues from wine and spirits exports. https://wine.gov.ge/En/News/38181
citations.title: Record revenues from wine and spirits exports
citations.summary: Official 2024 export results for Georgian wine and spirits, including volume, revenue, and destination-country growth.
citations.relevance: Provides the export-exposure baseline used to justify why counterfeit risk and verification matter at border and distributor level.

citations.apa: National Wine Agency of Georgia. (2023, June 6). The 8,000-year-old uninterrupted tradition of wine production in Georgia. https://wine.gov.ge/En/News/37074
citations.title: The 8,000-year-old uninterrupted tradition of wine production in Georgia
citations.summary: National Wine Agency background on Georgia's long viticulture history and its large base of indigenous grape varieties.
citations.relevance: Supports the paper's cultural and biodiversity rationale for starting the provenance pilot in Georgia.

citations.apa: UNESCO. (2013). Decision of the Intergovernmental Committee: Traditional Georgian winemaking method in qvevri. https://ich.unesco.org/en/decisions/8.COM/8.13
citations.title: Traditional Georgian winemaking method in qvevri
citations.summary: UNESCO recognition of qvevri winemaking as an element of intangible cultural heritage.
citations.relevance: Anchors the whitepaper's claim that Georgian wine identity is not only commercial but also cultural and institutional.

citations.apa: National Intellectual Property Center of Georgia (Sakpatenti). (2018, August 9). Counterfeit wine labeled with appellation of origin 'Kindzmarauli' revealed in Russia. https://www.sakpatenti.gov.ge/en/news/5309/
authors: National Intellectual Property Center of Georgia (Sakpatenti)
citations.title: Counterfeit wine labeled with appellation of origin 'Kindzmarauli' revealed in Russia
citations.summary: Cross-border enforcement case involving counterfeit bottles using a protected Georgian appellation.
citations.relevance: Shows that Georgian wine counterfeiting is documented and enforceable, not a hypothetical future risk.

citations.apa: National Wine Agency of Georgia. (2018, December 31). Counterfeit Georgian wine batch removed from sale in Ukraine. https://wine.gov.ge/En/News/25735
citations.title: Counterfeit Georgian wine batch removed from sale in Ukraine
citations.summary: Official notice that counterfeit Georgian wine was identified and removed from the Ukrainian market.
citations.relevance: Supports the paper's argument that verification has to work across jurisdictions and import markets.

citations.apa: World Intellectual Property Organization. (1999). Law of Georgia on appellations of origin and geographical indications of goods. WIPO Lex. https://www.wipo.int/wipolex/en/text/217274
citations.title: Law of Georgia on appellations of origin and geographical indications of goods
citations.summary: The Georgian legal framework governing appellations of origin and geographical indications.
citations.relevance: Establishes that Terroir complements an existing GI regime rather than replacing it with a technical system.

citations.apa: World Intellectual Property Organization. (2025, February 25). Georgia strengthens the protection of its geographical indications. https://www.wipo.int/web/wipo-magazine/articles/georgia-strengthens-the-protection-of-its-geographical-indications-67406
citations.title: Georgia strengthens the protection of its geographical indications
citations.summary: WIPO coverage of Georgia's continuing international GI protection posture.
citations.relevance: Shows institutional momentum around origin protection and strengthens the case for digital enforcement accelerators.

citations.apa: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development. (2022). Global trade in fakes: A worrying threat. OECD Publishing. https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/global-trade-in-fakes-a-worrying-threat_74c81154-en.html
authors: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development
citations.title: Global trade in fakes: A worrying threat
citations.summary: OECD synthesis of the scale, channels, and enforcement implications of counterfeit trade.
citations.relevance: Provides the macro-level trade context for why authenticity infrastructure has real economic value.

citations.apa: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development, & European Union Intellectual Property Office. (2025). Mapping global trade in fakes 2025: Global trends and enforcement challenges. OECD Publishing. https://doi.org/10.1787/94d3b29f-en
authors: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development
citations.title: Mapping global trade in fakes 2025: Global trends and enforcement challenges
citations.summary: Joint OECD and EUIPO report on counterfeit flows, market trends, and enforcement conditions.
citations.relevance: Supports the whitepaper's claim that counterfeit risk is structurally cross-border and increasingly sophisticated.

citations.apa: National Wine Agency of Georgia. (2020). Annual report 2020. https://wine.gov.ge/En/Files/Download/12996
citations.title: Annual report 2020
citations.summary: National Wine Agency report covering vintage accounting, quality-control reforms, and lab methods used to detect falsification.
citations.relevance: Provides direct evidence that Terroir can align with existing transparency and lab-control workflows instead of inventing a parallel bureaucracy.

citations.apa: Ministry of Environmental Protection and Agriculture of Georgia. (2023, November 17). More than 5,800 vineyard owners received state assistance after hail damage in Kakheti. https://mepa.gov.ge/Ge/News/Details/21399
authors: Ministry of Environmental Protection and Agriculture of Georgia
citations.title: More than 5,800 vineyard owners received state assistance after hail damage in Kakheti
citations.summary: Official account of one-time aid distributed to vineyard owners after September 2023 hail events in Kakheti.
citations.relevance: Establishes the concrete climate-shock response case that the upcoming Shield module is designed to improve.

citations.apa: Rural Development Agency. (2025). Agroinsurance. https://www.rda.gov.ge/en/projects/Agroinsurance
citations.title: Agroinsurance
citations.summary: Program page describing Georgia's subsidized agricultural insurance framework, including coverage areas and co-financing.
citations.relevance: Shows that Georgia already has a policy and operational substrate for risk-transfer collaboration, making Shield more realistic as a next module.

citations.apa: World Bank. (2022). Index insurance for agricultural resilience: Promises, challenges and solutions. https://openknowledge.worldbank.org/entities/publication/1be2d7c8-9b22-5753-bf07-4a0df44bc09d
citations.title: Index insurance for agricultural resilience: Promises, challenges and solutions
citations.summary: World Bank synthesis on why index-based and parametric mechanisms help, where they fail, and how basis risk should be treated.
citations.relevance: Provides the conceptual backbone for the Shield trigger, payout, and basis-risk sections.

citations.apa: World Bank. (2019). Is index insurance a viable solution for disaster risk financing in agriculture? https://openknowledge.worldbank.org/entities/publication/e6cc66f4-a4b3-5355-bded-ef17f4f9d4b3
citations.title: Is index insurance a viable solution for disaster risk financing in agriculture?
citations.summary: World Bank note on operational design, index quality, and implementation limits of agricultural index insurance.
citations.relevance: Supports the decision to frame Shield first as an auditable relief and resilience layer rather than a fully regulated insurance product.

citations.apa: Moriondo, M., Ferrise, R., Dibari, C., and colleagues. (2024). Climate resilience pathways for European wine regions. Nature Food, 5, 551-562. https://www.nature.com/articles/s43016-024-00988-w
citations.title: Climate resilience pathways for European wine regions
citations.summary: Peer-reviewed assessment of climate risk and adaptation pathways across wine-producing regions.
citations.relevance: Gives scientific grounding to the whitepaper's claim that resilience data belongs alongside provenance data in vineyard-adjacent infrastructure.

citations.apa: Paliwal, V., Chandra, S., & Sharma, S. (2021). Blockchain technology for sustainable supply chain management: A systematic literature review and a classification framework. Trends in Food Science & Technology, 109, 637-660. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tifs.2021.08.012
citations.title: Blockchain technology for sustainable supply chain management
citations.summary: Systematic review of blockchain architectures, benefits, and implementation pitfalls in supply-chain settings.
citations.relevance: Supports the paper's selective, minimal on-chain strategy instead of a maximalist blockchain narrative.

citations.apa: Banstola, A., Ghimeray, A. K., & Ahmad, T. (2025). Do farmers participate in traceability systems? A systematic review and future directions. Journal of the Science of Food and Agriculture, 105(4), 2036-2049. https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/joss.70034
citations.title: Do farmers participate in traceability systems? A systematic review and future directions
citations.summary: Systematic review focused on farmer adoption incentives, frictions, and governance conditions in traceability systems.
citations.relevance: Supports the paper's operational requirement that Trace remain batch-first, low-friction, and aligned to existing workflows.

citations.apa: Digital Public Goods Alliance. (2025). DPG Standard. https://www.digitalpublicgoods.net/standard/
citations.title: DPG Standard
citations.summary: The standard used to assess whether a project qualifies as a digital public good.
citations.relevance: Gives a concrete governance target for Terroir as open infrastructure rather than a closed vendor product.

citations.apa: United Nations Development Programme. (2024). Default to open. https://www.undp.org/publications/default-open
citations.title: Default to open
citations.summary: UNDP publication arguing for open digital systems, reusable infrastructure, and public-interest software.
citations.relevance: Supports the whitepaper's insistence that trust infrastructure should remain forkable, inspectable, and reusable.

citations.apa: UNICEF Office of Innovation. (2026). Venture Fund. https://www.unicefinnovationfund.org/
citations.title: Venture Fund
citations.summary: Official UNICEF innovation funding surface describing equity-free support for open-source and frontier-technology ventures.
citations.relevance: Supports the paper's funder-facing positioning and explains why open-source infrastructure language matters.

citations.apa: UNICEF USA. (2026). Gift acceptance policy. https://www.unicefusa.org/about/finances-and-accountability/gift-acceptance-policy
citations.title: Gift acceptance policy
citations.summary: Policy page describing sensitive and excluded funding relationships, including alcohol-company restrictions.
citations.relevance: Explains why the paper frames Terroir as rural livelihoods and resilience infrastructure, not alcohol promotion.

figures.title: System overview: Trace now, Shield next
figures.caption: Terroir is structured as a layered trust stack. Trace is the current provenance and verification module. Shield is the planned resilience and auditable-response layer built on top of the same issuer, event, and evidence foundations.
figures.alt: Diagram showing Trace as the current product and Shield as the upcoming resilience module in the same infrastructure stack.

figures.title: Georgian wine export exposure in 2024
figures.caption: Official National Wine Agency figures show the scale and cross-border spread of the Georgian wine market, the exact environment in which counterfeit and authenticity disputes become costly.
figures.alt: Bar chart showing 2024 Georgian wine export volume, revenue, and selected market growth indicators.

figures.title: Where trust fails across the export chain
figures.caption: The problem is not production; it is maintaining trusted evidence of origin, handling, and authenticity as custody spreads across actors and jurisdictions.
figures.alt: Chain diagram from producer to importer showing risk points for counterfeit, relabeling, and record fragmentation.

figures.title: Trace data model
figures.caption: The Trace module binds producer, batch, evidence, attestation, and verification-proof objects into one hybrid record system with selective on-chain commitments.
figures.alt: Entity relationship style diagram showing producers, batches, evidence, attestations, scans, and verification proofs.

figures.title: Attestation and verification sequence
figures.caption: Trace only needs a small set of on-chain actions. Most operational data stays off-chain, while content hashes and issuer actions remain publicly verifiable.
figures.alt: Sequence diagram showing batch creation, evidence upload, attestation, hash anchoring, and public verification.

figures.title: Physical-digital binding and anomaly detection
figures.caption: QR is sufficient for low-friction transparency. Higher-risk export lines can add secure NFC and duplicate-scan logic without changing the core attestation model.
figures.alt: Diagram showing product tag scan flow with QR and NFC options and duplicate-scan anomaly alerting.

figures.title: Shield event-to-payout flow
figures.caption: Shield is designed as a planned resilience layer: weather data enters a trigger engine, oracles sign event claims, payout rules resolve against enrolled vineyard records, and results are publicly reported without exposing personal identities.
figures.alt: Workflow diagram showing weather event ingestion, oracle signing, trigger evaluation, payout execution, and public reporting.

figures.title: Illustrative parametric payout curve
figures.caption: A threshold, ramp, and cap keep the mechanism interpretable and auditable. The curve is illustrative rather than a production actuarial schedule.
figures.alt: Line chart of event severity versus payout showing threshold, payout ramp, and capped maximum.

figures.title: Basis risk as the core design constraint
figures.caption: The gap between realized loss and index payout is the central governance and design problem for Shield. That gap must be visible, measured, and monitored.
figures.alt: Comparison chart showing observed losses and parametric payouts diverging across multiple events.

figures.title: Rollout roadmap
figures.caption: The proposed rollout starts with batch passports for export SKUs, then layers in issuer integrations, anomaly analytics, and finally the forthcoming Shield resilience workflow.
figures.alt: Phased roadmap from prototype to pilot to institutional rollout and multi-product expansion.

tables.title: Problem-to-feature mapping
tables.caption: Trace addresses trust failures first. Shield extends the same trust primitives into climate-response operations once the evidence layer is stable.
rows: Copied labels, ambiguous appellation claims, refill risk
Product passport, issuer attestations, public verify page
Paper certificates, siloed databases, delayed export checks
Hybrid registry, canonical schemas, open APIs
Hash commitments, scan history, attestation chain
Slow assessments, opaque payout decisions, dispute risk
Weather trigger engine, audited relief workflow, public reporting
Support programs are hard to target and hard to audit
Enrollment records, milestone evidence, transparent disbursement logs

tables.title: Source-of-truth matrix by actor and claim type
tables.caption: Terroir works only when claims are assigned to the actors best placed to make them and when higher-trust claims can be distinguished from self-attested claims.
rows: Signed event packet and threshold log

equations.title: Trace commitment

variables.meaning: Batch content hash anchored or stored for verification.
Deterministic serialization of the batch fields that define provenance at registration time.

equations.interpretation: The batch hash is a compact commitment to the important provenance fields. If any of those fields change later, the verifier recomputes a different hash and the mismatch becomes visible.

equations.title: Attestation commitment

variables.meaning: Attestation hash stored as the public commitment.
Identifier of the batch being attested.
Credentialed issuer or signer identity.
The claim class, such as origin verification or lab result.
Structured evidence payload supporting the claim.

equations.interpretation: Trace does not anchor raw evidence on-chain. It anchors a cryptographic commitment that links the issuer, the claim type, and the structured payload into one verifiable record.

equations.title: Verification predicate

variables.meaning: Binary verification result for the batch record.
Indicator function equal to 1 when the stated condition is true.
Product over all attestations associated with the batch.

equations.interpretation: A batch verifies only when the off-chain batch hash matches the on-chain commitment and every attestation hash also matches its recorded commitment.

equations.title: Attestation-weight score

variables.meaning: Composite trust score for a batch or claim bundle.
Weight assigned to attestation class i based on issuer credibility and governance rules.
Observed attestation validity or presence for issuer class i.

equations.interpretation: The score is not a truth oracle. It is a way to summarize whether a claim is only self-reported or supported by stronger, independent attestations such as labs or authorities.

equations.title: Scan anomaly score
equations.expression: A(\text{tag}) = w_1 v_{\text{geo}} + w_2 v_{\text{time}} + w_3 v_{\text{dup}} + w_4 v_{\text{route}}

variables.meaning: Risk score associated with a specific QR or NFC tag.
Geographic inconsistency feature, such as impossible distance between scans.
Temporal inconsistency feature.
Duplicate or repeated scan pattern intensity.
Mismatch between observed and expected distribution path.

equations.interpretation: Anomaly scoring does not prove fraud by itself. It helps brand owners, importers, and authorities prioritize which suspicious events deserve review.

equations.title: Shield trigger indicator

variables.meaning: Observed hail or hazard intensity for the covered area.
Observed frost condition indicator.
Minimum temperature in the relevant event window.
Governance-approved thresholds for the program.

equations.interpretation: Shield uses transparent threshold rules so participants know in advance what event levels are sufficient to move a case into the payout workflow.

equations.title: Illustrative payout rule

variables.meaning: Payout for participant i.
Program cap for a single enrolled unit.
Covered area or another exposure measure for participant i.
Weights for hail, frost, and drought index components.
A drought or dryness indicator where relevant to the program.

equations.interpretation: The formula illustrates a transparent structure rather than a final actuarial contract. It shows how exposure, threshold exceedance, and capped support can be combined without hiding logic inside manual discretion.

equations.title: Basis risk measure

variables.meaning: Expected basis risk over observed cases.
Realized loss for participant i.
Program payout for participant i.
Expectation across cases or seasons.

equations.interpretation: Basis risk is the expected gap between real damage and parametric payout. Shield should make that gap visible and governable rather than pretending it disappears.
